title: Public Speaking & Building External Influence as a Staff+ Engineer
description: Building an external reputation through tech talks, conference presentations, technical writing, and open source contributions is a force multiplier for Staff+ engineers. This article covers how to design compelling talks, get accepted at conferences, write blog posts that build reputation, and create an external presence that amplifies your internal influence — with specific guidance for SAP engineers starting from zero.

sections.title: Why External Influence Matters at the Staff+ Level

description.value:
At FAANG companies, external influence is not a nice-to-have — it is explicitly or implicitly part of the Staff+ engineer job description. Google's L6+ promotion criteria include "external impact" as a distinct dimension. Meta's E6+ bar requires evidence that you "influence the industry." Amazon's Principal Engineer level expects you to be "broadly recognized" across the company and increasingly across the industry. The reason is straightforward: a Staff+ engineer who can only influence their immediate team has a leverage ceiling. An engineer who can publish a blog post read by 10,000 engineers, deliver a conference talk that shapes how an entire community thinks about a problem, or contribute to an open source project used by millions — that engineer's leverage multiplies exponentially.

SAP engineers come from a fundamentally different culture. At SAP, external visibility is often viewed with suspicion — it looks like you are not focused on the customer, or worse, that you are building personal brand at the company's expense. Customer presentations and SAP-internal events (DKOM, SAP TechEd) are the expected channels. Speaking at non-SAP conferences, writing on personal blogs, or contributing to non-SAP OSS projects is unusual and sometimes actively discouraged. This cultural conditioning is the single biggest obstacle SAP engineers face when transitioning to FAANG. The mental shift required is not small: you must unlearn the belief that visibility is selfish and internalize the belief that visibility is how Staff+ engineers multiply their impact.

There is a concrete ROI calculation. A single conference talk creates an artifact that you can reference for two years in performance reviews, promotion packets, and interviews. A well-written technical blog post remains relevant for three to five years. An open source contribution — especially to a widely-used project — is an artifact that follows you for your entire career. Compare that to an internal-only project, whose documentation and artifacts rarely survive beyond a quarter after you change teams. The compounding effect is even more powerful: external reputation leads to invitations to speak, which builds more reputation, which creates better job opportunities, which strengthens your negotiation position, which gives you more internal influence, which lets you take on bigger projects, which gives you more things to talk about externally. Each cycle reinforces the next.

For SAP engineers specifically, the unique perspective you bring is itself the differentiator. FAANG-native engineers have deep knowledge of microservices, Kubernetes, and modern distributed systems — but they have almost no experience with enterprise-scale monolithic systems, regulatory compliance at SAP's scale, 30-year-old codebases that process billions in transactions daily, or the human dynamics of migrating entire industries off legacy platforms. These topics are not only interesting to FAANG audiences — they are rare. A talk like "How We Migrated 10 Million SAP Records Without a Single Minute of Downtime" will stand out in a CFP review pile filled with yet another Kubernetes operator talk. Your enterprise experience is an asset, not a liability. The key is translating it into language that resonates outside the SAP ecosystem.

sections.title: Tech Talk Design — From Zero to Conference Ready

description.value:
Most SAP engineers have never given a public tech talk. Internal presentations to customers and stakeholders do not count — those are sales or status updates dressed in slides. A tech talk is different: it teaches something new, shares a hard-won lesson, or challenges conventional wisdom. The first step is finding your talk idea. The best talks solve a real problem the speaker encountered. Do not try to cover everything. The single biggest mistake first-time speakers make is trying to cram too much content into 30 minutes. Pick one problem, one solution, and one takeaway. A good heuristic: if you cannot explain your talk in one sentence, you do not have a talk yet.

Writing the abstract is where most CFPs are won or lost. A great abstract has four parts. The hook: one or two sentences that make the reader think "I need to know this." Often this is a surprising number — "We reduced a 4-hour batch job to 47 seconds" — or a provocative question — "What if I told you your database migration strategy is backwards?" The problem: two to three sentences describing the specific challenge you faced, with enough technical detail to establish credibility but not so much that it loses the reader. The solution: a brief overview of what you built or discovered, emphasizing the approach rather than the implementation details. The takeaway: what the audience will walk away with. Be specific: "You will learn three strangler fig patterns for migrating monoliths without downtime" is much better than "You will learn about migration patterns."

Slide design follows a few principles that are surprisingly universal across FAANG. One idea per slide — if you find yourself putting bullet point four on a slide, that bullet point needs its own slide. No walls of text — the audience should be able to read the slide in three seconds. Use diagrams instead of bullets wherever possible. A well-designed architecture diagram communicates more in five seconds than a paragraph communicates in thirty. Use code snippets sparingly and only when the code itself is the point. Your slides are a visual aid for the audience, not a script for you. You should be able to give the talk without looking at your slides at all. If you need slide text to remember what to say, you have not rehearsed enough.

Rehearsal is where good talks become great talks. Record yourself giving the talk and watch it back. It will be uncomfortable. Do it anyway. You will notice verbal tics (um, uh, like, you know), pacing problems (rushing through the interesting part, dwelling on the setup), and moments where your explanation is less clear than you thought. Present to a friendly audience — your team, a mentor, a peer — and ask for specific feedback: "What was the most interesting part? What was confusing? What would you cut?" Time your talk repeatedly. A 30-minute talk that runs 35 minutes is a failure. Conference slots are rigid; running over time is disrespectful to the next speaker and the audience. Cut ruthlessly. If a slide is not essential, delete it. If an anecdote takes 90 seconds but only adds 30 seconds of value, cut it.

Handling Q&A is a skill separate from delivering the talk. Anticipate questions and prepare answers. It is perfectly acceptable to say "I don't know" — in fact, it builds credibility. The worst Q&A move is blustering through an answer to a question you do not understand. If a question is unclear, ask clarifying questions. If a question is off-topic, say "That's a great question, but it's outside the scope of this talk. Let's discuss it after." If a question is hostile (rare, but it happens), stay calm and professional. Do not get defensive. Thank them for the question and answer what you can. The audience is on your side — they showed up to learn from you.

For SAP engineers, specific talk ideas that would resonate at FAANG conferences include: "Strangler Fig Patterns for 30-Year-Old Monoliths" (drawing from SAP system migration experience), "Compliance as Code: Lessons from Enterprise ERP" (how SAP handles regulatory requirements that most FAANG engineers never encounter), "The Human Side of Legacy Migration" (organizational change management at enterprise scale), "Operating a Billion-Dollar System: What Distributed Systems Theory Misses" (the gap between textbook CAP theorem and real-world enterprise operations), and "Why Eventual Consistency Is Terrifying When Money Is on the Line" (SAP's approach to transactional consistency and what modern systems can learn from it).

Getting accepted at a conference is a pipeline, not a lottery. The most reliable path starts with internal FAANG conferences. Every large tech company has internal engineering conferences: Google has Solve (formerly Google Engineering Conference) and internal Tech Talks, Meta has @Scale internal sessions and Tech Talks, Amazon has internal engineering forums and principal engineer summits, Apple has internal engineering reviews, Netflix has internal tech talks. These are the lowest-friction opportunities to give your first talk. The audience is internal, the stakes are lower, and the feedback is direct. Submit to every internal CFP that matches your expertise. One great internal talk can lead to invitations to speak at other internal events across the company.

The external conference landscape divides into tiers. Tier 1 — local meetups and user groups: these are the easiest to get into and the best practice. Meetup organizers are desperate for speakers. If you have never given a talk, start here. Tier 2 — regional conferences: events like LISA, SREcon, QCon, and regional cloud conferences. These are more competitive but still accessible to first-time speakers with a strong proposal. Tier 3 — major industry conferences: Strange Loop, KubeCon, Google I/O, AWS re:Invent, MongoDB World, and the O'Reilly conferences. These are highly competitive. Plan for a 10-20% acceptance rate on your first submissions. Do not be discouraged by rejections — even Staff+ engineers with years of speaking experience get rejected regularly.

The CFP submission is a specific skill. Most CFPs are judged in under two minutes. The title and abstract do 80% of the work. Write a title that is specific and intriguing — "Strangler Fig in Practice: Migrating a 30-Year-Old Monolith" is better than "Legacy Migration Patterns." Use the abstract format described in the previous section. Avoid buzzwords — "leveraging AI-driven synergies" is an instant rejection signal. Be honest about your experience level. You do not need to be a world expert to give a talk. "I encountered this problem, I solved it, here is what I learned" is a perfectly valid premise. Include a speaker bio that establishes credibility without being boring. "Staff+ engineer working on distributed systems at Google" is fine. "I once debugged a production issue that turned out to be a cosmic ray bit flip" is better — it shows personality.

FAANG companies actively sponsor conference speaking. Google has a formal conference speaker approval process that can cover travel, accommodation, and registration. Meta and Amazon similarly have budgets for employee speaking at industry events. The process typically requires manager approval and sometimes a review of the talk content for proprietary information. The key framing for your manager: "This talk builds our recruiting brand in the SAP migration space" or "Speaking at this conference increases our visibility with enterprise customers." Frame it as company benefit, not personal benefit. Most FAANG managers understand that speaking is part of Staff+ career development and will approve reasonable requests.

Networking at conferences should be approached differently as a Staff+ engineer. Do not try to collect business cards or LinkedIn connections. Aim for meaningful conversations with 3-5 people per day. Before the conference, identify talks and speakers you want to connect with. Prepare specific questions about their work. After a talk, approach the speaker with a thoughtful question or observation — "I loved your point about X. At my company, we found Y when we tried that approach. Have you seen that?" This starts a real conversation rather than a transactional interaction. Follow up within 48 hours with a personalized message referencing your conversation. The goal is not to build a network of weak connections — it is to build a network of people who remember you as someone who does interesting work and asks good questions.

The specific pathway for SAP engineers is clear. Start with SAP-themed talks at internal FAANG events. Talk about your SAP migration, your enterprise integration challenges, your compliance experiences. Then expand to distributed systems topics that draw from your SAP experience but are framed in universal terms. Local meetups are the next step — most cities have meetups for Java, distributed systems, cloud infrastructure, and site reliability. Submit to one meetup per month for six months. By the end of that period, you will have 3-6 talks under your belt, plus recordings and feedback. Then submit to a Tier 2 conference. By year two, you should be submitting to major conferences and building a reputation in your niche.

items.label: Clear hook in the first sentence — why should anyone care?
Problem statement — what specific problem does this talk address?
Solution overview — what did you build/learn/discover?
Key takeaway — what will the audience know that they didn't before?
Speaker bio that establishes credibility without being boring
Previous talk experience mentioned (or pivot: "I've taught this to my team")

description.value:
Writing is the highest-leverage external influence activity for a Staff+ engineer. A single well-written technical blog post can reach more engineers than years of internal presentations. At FAANG, internal blogging is the starting point. Google's internal engineering blog (colloquially known as the "testblog" or internal Blogger instance) is where many Staff+ engineers build their first external-style writing muscles. Meta's internal Workplace engineering groups serve the same purpose. These internal platforms let you practice writing for an engineering audience with lower stakes and immediate feedback from colleagues. Do not skip this step. Write 3-5 internal posts before publishing anything externally. Internal writing teaches you the voice and structure that works for your company's engineering culture without the pressure of public scrutiny.

The anatomy of a great technical blog post follows a structure that has proven effective across FAANG engineering blogs. The headline should state the problem and the result: "How We Reduced Our CI Pipeline from 45 Minutes to 8 Minutes" or "Migrating 10 Million Records Without Downtime: A Case Study in Strangler Fig Patterns." The subtitle adds context: "Lessons learned, tradeoffs made, and what we would do differently." The body follows a proven sequence. Start with the problem — describe what was not working, why it mattered, and how you knew it was a problem (include data). Then cover what you tried — Option A and why it failed, Option B and why it partially worked, Option C and why you ultimately chose it. This narrative of exploration is more compelling than a simple solution description. Then present the solution in detail — include an architecture diagram, key code snippets (never proprietary code, but enough to be useful), and metrics before and after. Close with tradeoffs and learnings — what you would do differently, what surprised you, and when someone should not use this approach.

Internal vs external writing requires different levels of polish. Internal writing can be more candid: you can name specific teams, mention internal tools by their internal names, and be honest about organizational challenges. External writing requires more filtering: remove proprietary details, rename internal tools to generic descriptions, and frame organizational challenges as universal lessons rather than company-specific stories. Do not lie or obscure the truth — but do not expose confidential information. A good rule: if you would not say it in a job interview, do not write it in an external post. Most FAANG companies require engineering blog posts to go through a legal review process. Build this into your timeline — legal review can take 2-6 weeks.

SAP engineers should start with internal posts about distributed systems patterns they encounter in their SAP work. "How We Handle Stateful Transactions Across 12 Systems" is a great internal post topic. "The Curious Case of the Phantom Deadlock: Debugging ABAP and Java Interoperability" is another. After 3-5 internal posts, graduate to external writing. Your first external posts should repurpose your best internal content, removing proprietary details and reframing the lessons for a general engineering audience. The first external post is the hardest. After that, the process becomes routine. Aim for one external post per month. This cadence is sustainable alongside a full-time engineering role and builds momentum without burnout. Consistency matters more than virality. A post that gets 500 reads every month for two years has more impact than a post that gets 10,000 reads in one week and is never seen again.

Distribution is the part of writing that most engineers neglect. Writing the post is only half the work. Share it on LinkedIn with a 2-3 sentence summary of the key insight and a question to spark discussion. Share it on Twitter/X with a compelling pull quote and a link. If your company has an engineering blog program, submit it there. Cross-post to Medium or Dev.to if your employer allows. Hacker News is unpredictable — you cannot reliably trigger a front-page post, but if it happens, be prepared for intense, often critical discussion. Do not respond defensively. Thank commenters for their engagement and incorporate good feedback. The most important distribution channel is word of mouth. If one senior engineer at another company reads your post and forwards it to their team, that is worth more than 100 random reads. Write for that one senior engineer, not for the masses.

sections.title: Internal Brand Building — Tech Talks, Demos, and Office Hours

description.value:
Before you build an external reputation, build one inside your company. Internal brand building is the training ground for external influence. It is also valuable in its own right — a strong internal reputation as the go-to person for a topic creates opportunities for larger projects, better reviews, and faster promotions. At FAANG companies, internal reputation is currency. The engineer who gives a great internal tech talk becomes the person others recommend when a new project needs a lead. The engineer who writes clear internal docs becomes the person others ask to review their design documents. The engineer who holds office hours becomes the person junior engineers seek out for mentorship — and mentorship is one of the most visible Staff+ signals.

Internal tech talks are the fastest way to build reputation. Start with lunch-and-learns for your immediate team. A 20-minute talk about something you worked on recently — a tricky bug, a performance optimization, a migration pattern — establishes you as someone with valuable knowledge to share. Expand to team-wide or org-wide presentations. Most FAANG companies have regular internal tech talk series or "brown bags." Volunteer to speak at these. A single great org-wide talk can make your name known to hundreds of engineers. After the talk, share your slides and recording on internal channels. Follow up with a written summary. This creates the artifact that continues working for you long after the talk is over.

Office hours are an underutilized internal influence tool. Offer a recurring 30-minute slot where anyone can ask you questions about your area of expertise. For SAP engineers at FAANG, this is particularly powerful. You are likely one of a small number of engineers with deep SAP knowledge. Offer office hours for "SAP Integration Patterns," "Enterprise Migration Strategy," or "Legacy System Modernization." You will be surprised how many engineers have questions they have been sitting on. Office hours build relationships across the company, surface problems you would not otherwise hear about, and establish you as a recognized authority. The investment is one hour per week. The return is disproportionate.

Internal blog posts amplify everything else. After you give a talk, write a post summarizing it. After you solve a complex problem, write a post about the approach. After you complete a migration, write a post-mortem that others can learn from. Internal blogs at FAANG are widely read — engineers use them to learn what is happening across the company, find solutions to problems they are encountering, and identify experts they can reach out to. A strong internal blogging presence can make you the recognized expert in a topic across a company of 100,000+ engineers. For SAP engineers, internal blogging about SAP-related challenges and solutions is particularly valuable because the expertise is scarce and in demand.

Specific internal talk topics for SAP engineers that would resonate at FAANG include: "Lessons from Operating a Billion-Dollar Legacy System" (framing SAP experience as operating at extreme scale under extreme constraints), "How We Migrated 10 Million Records Without Downtime" (the strangler fig pattern applied to enterprise data), "The Compliance Tax: What Enterprise Regulations Cost in Engineering Time" (making the case for compliance automation), "Why Your Distributed System Won't Work for Enterprise Customers" (the gap between modern architecture and enterprise requirements), and "What SAP Gets Right That FAANG Gets Wrong" (thoughtful contrarian takes that demonstrate intellectual honesty).

sections.title: The SAP Engineer's External Influence Path

description.value:
This is a concrete, month-by-month roadmap for an SAP engineer starting from zero external presence. The timeline assumes you have a full-time engineering job and are building external influence on the side. Do not quit your job or sacrifice performance to build external presence. Consistency and sustainability matter more than intensity.

Months 1-3: Foundation. Start an internal blog and write three posts about your current project. Each post should be 500-1000 words, focused on a specific problem and solution. Do not worry about perfection — internal blogs have lower standards. Give one internal tech talk to your immediate team. It can be 15 minutes. The goal is not brilliance — it is to break the psychological barrier of "I am not ready to speak." Find an open source project your team uses and make two small contributions (documentation fixes, bug reproductions, test improvements). Do not aim for major features yet. The goal is to practice the OSS contribution workflow. Set up a personal blog on a simple platform (Medium, Dev.to, or a static site). Do not publish anything yet. Just have the infrastructure ready.

Months 4-6: First external steps. Write two external blog posts. Repurpose your best internal content — remove proprietary details, reframe for a general audience, add a stronger narrative hook. Submit a CFP to a local meetup. The worst case is rejection, which gives you practice writing proposals. The best case is acceptance, which gives you your first external speaking slot. Continue OSS contributions — aim for five more contributions, slightly increasing in complexity. Read the contribution guidelines for one major project you use and identify a small feature or bug fix you can implement. Expand your internal brand: give one talk to a broader audience (org-wide or cross-team), add one more internal blog post, and consider starting office hours.

Months 7-12: Building momentum. Speak at two local meetups. The first will be nerve-wracking. The second will be easier. Record both talks if possible — recordings become artifacts. Write one article for a major publication. Options include: your company's engineering blog (if they have a program), a publication like InfoQ, or a high-profile personal post that you promote through LinkedIn and Twitter. Make ten more OSS contributions across 2-3 projects. By now, you should have a basic OSS presence: a few merged PRs, some issue comments, and ideally one meaningful feature or bug fix. Submit a CFP to a Tier 2 conference (QCon, SREcon, LISA, regional KubeCon). Do not be discouraged by rejection — most speakers get rejected 2-3 times before their first conference acceptance.

Year 2 and beyond: Recognition. Submit to a major conference (Strange Loop, KubeCon, Google I/O, AWS re:Invent). By now, you have 3-6 talks under your belt, 8-12 blog posts, and a growing OSS footprint. Your CFP proposals will be stronger because you have real experience. Establish a regular writing cadence: one external post per month. Maintain your internal presence: one internal talk per quarter, regular office hours, internal blog posts as projects warrant. Become a recognized voice in your niche. By the end of year two, you should be one of the go-to external voices on enterprise migration, legacy modernization, or distributed systems at SAP scale. This reputation will open doors: speaking invitations, consulting requests, job opportunities, and promotion committees that cite your external impact as evidence of Staff+ level influence.

The key insight: SAP engineers have a unique perspective that FAANG-native engineers lack. Experience with enterprise-scale systems, compliance regimes, legacy migration, and long-lived codebases is rare in the FAANG world. Most FAANG engineers have never dealt with a system that has been running continuously for 20+ years, let alone one that processes billions of dollars in transactions daily. Your experience with SAP gives you depth in areas that are becoming increasingly relevant as FAANG companies move into enterprise markets, acquire enterprise customers, and build systems that need to operate at enterprise scale. This is not a liability — it is your competitive advantage. Lean into it.

value.title: Adversarial: "I Don't Have Anything Worth Talking About"
value.scenario: An SAP engineer believes their work is too mundane or specific to be interesting to a broader audience.

bad.label: Weak Response — Self-Rejection
bad.text: "You're right, my work on migration tooling probably isn't interesting enough for a conference."

good.label: Staff+ Response — Find the Universal Pattern
good.text: "Every problem you solve has a universal pattern underneath it. You migrated a legacy system? That's a distributed systems talk about strangler fig patterns. You optimized a slow query? That's a database performance talk. You dealt with a compliance requirement that blocked a deployment? That's an infrastructure talk about policy-as-code. The specific technology (SAP, HANA, ABAP) is just the wrapper. The core problem — migration, performance, compliance — is universal. Extract the pattern, remove the SAP-specific jargon, and you have a talk that any engineer in the audience will learn from."

details: Staff+ Insight: In SAP, external visibility is a career risk — it looks like you are not focused on the customer. At FAANG, external visibility is a career requirement — it proves you are a thought leader, not just a coder. Start small: one internal tech talk, one blog post. The compound interest of external reputation is the highest-ROI investment you can make as a Staff+ engineer.
